titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 27, September, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell expresses gratitude for a parcel he received containing sandwiches, apples, and pears.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
2pm 27/9/17 My dearest Mamy Well here I am back with the
battalion and they are still out resting
and in the same place that they went to
the day I left them. I had a very
comfortable journey all the way and
no delays anywhere. I went to see
Mr. & Mrs. Davison last night about
8pm and stayed chatting until 10. The are most delightful people
and were delighted to see me. They
had not got Daddy's letter up to
the time I was there but I knew that
would be so. I was not rough
enough crossing for me to make
any money in the way I said I hoped
to. You remember what that was. The sandwiches came in very
handy and also the apples and
pears. I met an officer I knew at
Kingstown and was with him all
the time till I got to France but then
he had to go by a different train. The partridge are in perfect order
and we are going to have them for
dinner tonight. There are no
changes whatever in the battalion
since I left. This is quite a decent village
and the billets very good indeed.
We may be here for quite a
time yet. I see another attack started
yesterday but there are no
particulars in yet about it Do
not expect a letter every day
while we are here because
we are just as safe as we
would be at home. Best love to all
including Nellie Your most affec Son
Willie
